sentences.text: I think we were very fortunate in having Miss Richman at the head of UNRRA.
Because--
Gertrude Richman, R-I-C-H-M-A-N.
She was a lady at that time, a social worker from Washington, age I would say in her late 40s.
Very, very vivacious, very interested in the survivors, and very sympathetic of their needs.
And I think it was her empathy which made Rosenheim run very efficiently.
The AJDC supplied a lot of the Jewish needs, but under-supplied the food.
Very, very good.
It was quite interesting.
The-- the supplies depended on the numbers of the camp.
And when we got in an extra group of children, we got extra supplies.
When a group of children were transferred to another camp, the numbers were less.
I don't know whether this is relevant or not, but during my stay in Germany and in Munich, rather in Rosenheim, I was asked by Seal, the AJDC Director, to go to another camp to supervise elections.
And really not elections so much as have a census of the number of people at a camp that was being opened.
Because some more had come through from-- from Eastern Europe.
And I was sent to this place.
I can't remember the place.
But it was a-- a small-- a small camp.
And the people who were doing the census were the displaced persons, one of the seniors of the displaced persons and AJDC.
This was towards the end of my stay, at about I would say in about December 1947.
And it was quite interesting.
Because it had-- it was at a place that I hadn't been before.
And it was very beautiful.
So the person who was in charge of the census, realizing that I was young and naive, said to me, why don't you look around the area.
It's very beautiful.
We'll do the counting, and we'll give you the numbers.
And not suspecting any foul play, I said, oh, that's fine.
So I took myself on a long, long walk to the lake, which was nearby, and spent most of the time meditating at the lake.
And when I came back, he said well we've counted all the people and this is the numbers.
And I brought these back to Miss Richman.
And we then took it to Seal in Munich.
Because it was under the AJDC.
And Seal looks at this and said, it's impossible.
We can't have so many people there.
It would be overcrowded.
I said, well, this is the figures he gave me.
And the reason why he gave me this inflated number was it would have meant that more food would have come to them.
I'm not sure what actually happened.
But this was a kind of, I suppose, a kind of-- I won't say dishonesty, but the kind of shrewdness that these people had.
They knew that they could get around a young, rather naive and innocent person by telling her to go and look around.
And they, I think, made an arbitrary number of-- for the census.
And that, of course, wasn't efficient.
I wasn't efficient.
I should have been more careful in seeing that everybody who registered was one, and not 10.
What actually happened was that there was a table, and-- and each person had to come and sign their name.
And that was how the statistics worked.
But what happened, and I heard that subsequently, was that one person would then go-- the person that had signed then would go to the end of the queue, and make up a fictitious name.
So that instead of having a supply, say for 500, there was supplies given for a thousand.
Because everybody had-- had signed their names twice, or other names twice.
Well, there-- there weren't really many family.
There were some families.
But as I said, most of the children were under the Madrichim.
And the whole idea of these-- these groups, was that the Jewish agency wanted to take them out of the camp through to Marseilles, and then on to-- Haifa, and illegally into Palestine.
And at the time, of course, British-- the British were in control of Palestine, and the number of immigrants that were allowed into Palestine was-- there was a limited number every month.
And what the Jewish agency often did was take them through to-- to Haifa, and get them in illegally, and perhaps one remembers which was a ship that was stopped by the British.
A lot of the children on originally came from Rosenheim.
They were taken from Rosenheim through to ship.
And ship was stopped, if you remember.
And the British sent them back.
And they went back to-- I think the British there-- it was under the British.
But the work of the Jewish agency was really to get as many people into Palestine as possible.
Because I think that they were hoping that Palestine would eventually become the state for the Jews.
But at that-- in 1946, '47, it was still a dream, not a reality.
What you mean by transportation services, the illegal or the legal?
Well, there was no really-- the children spent all their time in the camps.
They would perhaps go into Munich, and then they would use German transport.
There wasn't AJDC transport that would take them, or UNRRA transport, unless they were transferring one group of children to another camp for some reason.
And if-- if the children under the Madrichim wanted to go anywhere, they would take the German transport.
And since Rosenheim was about an hour and a half by road to Munich, they would have to get assistance from UNRRA.
And the UNRRA transport officer would allow them to take a transport of UNRRA.
Incidentally, the transport officer at Rosenheim was a man called Andy Sarason, who again, was an American who was working with UNRRA, who was a most wonderful man.
And he actually provided transport for [?
Umrum, ?] who was a Jewish agency chap, who would take the transport across borders illegally.
I don't think that Andy knew that they were going to go illegally.
But he never asked questions.
If [?
Umrum ?] said, I need it, I need a couple of trucks because I want to take some children to Munich, he would get the trucks, and [?
Umrum ?] would take them and transport them through to-- through borders, and leave them with people in Marseilles.
And then they would go illegally to Haifa.
And when [?
Umrum ?] brought the trucks back, there was never any questions by Andy.
He just assumed that [?
Umrum ?] knew what he was doing.
In ignorance, I thought they were happy.
I don't think they were unhappy.
I think that they had food.
They had clothing.
There was entertainment.
They had sports facilities.
They had the gym, sports to play soccer, and sports activities, athletics, took place.
In fact, the first athletic meetings, athletic races, took place at Rosenheim.
And this, the Jewish agency and myself, organized.
And the children loved it.
I think they were fairly happy.
They looked neglected.
And in fact, one of my first duties in-- in Rosenheim, was with the-- with the UNRRA, I went to collect them at the border, Polish border, and brought them to Rosenheim.
And when we arrived at the border-- border-- the children had to have DDT sprayed on them.
And I remember thinking, this is terrible.
It's like animals.
Why do we have to spray them?
I asked one of the UNRRA workers, why do we have to spray them like animals?
And the answer was because they are dirty.
Not because they were Jewish children, but because the conditions in Poland were still very primitive.
And they felt that it would be better to spray them, so that any infection or any bugs would be destroyed.
And once they came to Rosenheim, then they were given clothes from UNRRA and AJDC.
AJDC was-- was very generous, and so was UNRRA, in dressing the children well.
Oh, [LAUGHS] very much so.
I think it the-- it was the most lucrative business there was.
And I wasn't a smoker.
But all the American personnel, and we were in American officer's uniform.
We had access to what was called a PX, the stores, the army stores.
And we were given a ration of two cartons of cigarettes, which meant I think, 20, about 40 packets of cigarettes per week.

And since I wasn't a smoker, I either bought things that I wanted to send home on the black market, or I gave it to the people in the camps, the adults in the camps, who of course used it to buy themselves things on the black market.
It was a very lucrative market.
And I would say that most-- most of the survivors were in-- on the black market.
In fact, my-- my uncle and my cousin in Munich, were very big shots in the black market, the godfathers of the black market.
On the black market?
No, except that I think I wasn't the only one.
We also got chocolates at PX and coffee.
And I think the three most-- the three biggest shortages on the-- on the German market was first cigarettes, then coffee, then chocolates.
And if the displaced persons had any of those items, they could sell them on the black market to the Germans.
And they could get a lot of marks and even dollars.
Because a lot of the Germans would trade things for dollars, and then buy from the displaced persons.
No.
I think that it was after the Haganah period.
One of the girls in the Jewish agency who worked with us-- I can't remember her name because she left very early in my period in-- in Rosenheim-- she had been in the Haganah.
A young girl, I would say she was about 21 at the time.
So she must have been about 19.
And all the Palestinian Jewish agency people were idealists, who had only one-- one purpose.
And that was to get as many people across the borders into Palestine as possible.
Yes, I gave all my cigarettes.
All the-- all the things that they asked for, I gave it to them.
And I was very moral at the time.
And-- and I said that they-- you know, how dare they use it on the black market.
I'll give it to them on condition they use it for themselves, but not on the black market.
But of course I was very stupid to think that the things I gave them would be used for anything else but to exchange for things that they would need when they came to a new country.
And a lot of the displaced persons were able to accumulate quite a lot of worldly possessions for their future.
There was this one person, I think I mentioned him to you, [?
Misha. ?] Who was a [NON-ENGLISH] during the war.
He came from Russia.
And he met his wife in the displaced persons camp.
And they were very, very prominent in the camp.
And they got a lot of stuff, not only from me, from all the personnel.
And they were charming and very clever.
And by the time they left for Palestine, they had accumulated many, many, many items which they used once they got to Palestine.
And I would say that anyone who could do that, did it.
And now in retrospect, I think that's the way it should have been.
But at the age that I was, and the moral standing that I thought I had, I thought it was disgusting.
Yes.
There was a-- a-- a Gordon family.
And they came as a family.
And they left as a family.
And when they left, they came to say goodbye to me, and said that it was marvelous to know somebody who knew nothing about the experiences these people had, could understand and help them.
And they got to Palestine.
And they wrote to me in Rosenheim to say how grateful they were that I had given them so much.
I-- I used to give them cigarettes and chocolates.
The-- the children, I knew, ate the chocolates.
And the coffee and the cigarettes they did exchange for things that they needed.
But the children were really-- really well brought up.
And they loved the chocolate.
And then there was a young man called her Simcha Starec, who was a great gymnast.
And I think it was under his sort of pressure that I arranged for the gymnasium to be brought in by the Americans.
And then he became the gymnast.
He became the head of the gymnasium.
And he-- he was a-- I suppose a young man of about 20-- 23, 24 at the time.
He had come via Siberia to-- to Poland.
And then he-- none of his family survived.
And he came to Germany.
And he was very attached to me.
Because I somehow gave him a feeling that he was really worth something.
Because he became the director of the gymnasium, and he was in charge of the gymnastics for the schoolchildren.
And he gained a tremendous amount of confidence.
And when he left, and he left quite early through the underground, he came and he hugged me.
And he cried and he said, you're the only person that really helped me.
And now I feel human.
And I'm going to Palestine, and I'm going to live there like a human being again.
And many years later, or about 10 years later, when I went to-- it was then Israel-- I think in '49.
And he had written to me.
So He knew my address.
He came and met me at the airport.
And he was very, very, very grateful to see me.
And I was, of course, very happy to see him.
And he-- the first thing he said to me is, he says, I'm getting married.
And that was the best thing that I could have heard from him.
And then, of course, you know he took me around Israel.
And he introduced me to his fiancee.
And that was the end it.
I never heard from him again.
So I assume he was very happy.
No, not-- not when I-- I didn't have the address when I went to Israel.
But they did write to me from Israel.
And when I tried to get them at that address, they were no longer there, and nobody knew where they were.
They had been in-- in Siberia as a family group.
And they came to Rosenheim as a family group, one of the few families that came as a group.
Mother, father, and two children.
Well, the Eva and Misha were quite important to me as well.
And Eva gave birth to a child towards the end of their stay in-- in-- in Rosenheim.
And they left, and they went on So they had quite unpleasant experiences.
And they landed up in Cyprus.
And they eventually got to Israel.
And I did-- I was in touch with Misha, when I had one visit to Israel.
And he was practicing as a doctor.
And I said to him, Misha.
You're-- you're not a qualified doctor.
You were a [NON-ENGLISH].
And he said, well, we had no certificates.
We weren't asked for certificates.
And if I don't understand what's going on, then I refer them to a proper doctor.
A [NON-ENGLISH] was like a male nurse who was-- who knew quite a lot about medicine.
Because they acted as, I think, doctor substitutes in the Russian army when there weren't enough doctors to go around.
And he was quite knowledgeable.
And I think he was clever enough to realize that he couldn't practice as a genuine doctor.
So any-- if he wasn't-- if it was anything more serious then-- then a first aid thing, then he would refer them to a proper doctor.
And Eva was actually very depressed.
Because they lost their child.
I think they lost the child in Cyprus or somewhere.
I'm not quite sure of the details.
But the child didn't survive.
Oh, very well.
I was made a big fuss.
Because I was the-- the baby of the-- of the team.
And everybody was much more mature, much more worldly than me.
And I loved them, and they loved me.
I made a lot of friends with people from Europe, who were also attached to UNRRA.
UNRRA incorporated all the people who-- all the-- the victors, like the Norwegian.
There were some Norwegian, Danish, Holland, Belgium personnel attached to UNRRA, and of course Americans.
The Americans were the sort of elite.
But the Europeans were almost second-class, but they had all the privileges of UNRRA.
And we had a-- we lived in one of the Nazi homes, in a villa, it was a Nazi home.
And we had access to Berchtesgaden.
And [NON-ENGLISH], and in Berchtesgaden, we lived in a Nazi villa.
And when we looked out, we could actually see the Eagle's Nest.
The Eagle's Nest was the place where Hitler used to spend a lot of time.
And of course my first reaction to Berchtesgaden was in winter.
And it was so beautiful.
And I kept saying how could anyone be so cruel in such beautiful surroundings?
It was just impossible.
Because it's very picturesque, and very lovely.
My-- my closest friend was an American negress lady, who was with UNRRA.
And we traveled quite a lot together all over, and a British person attached to UNRRA.
The American lady was Erma Henry.
And the English lady was Daphne.
But I [LAUGHS] can't remember her surname.
But also one of the people that had a tremendous impact on me was a girl who came as a British worker, who was a psychologist.
But she had-- she was born in Vienna-- no, she was born in Prague.
And the family moved to Vienna.
And her parents realized that there were problems ahead.
So they sent her to London to study under Anna Freud.
So she became a psychologist under Anna Freud.
And she came to work in UNRRA.
And she was attached to our-- to the-- to the villa we were staying in.
And she was the UNRRA representative.
But Gertrude and I were very friendly, and we spent a lot of time together.
To get them out of Europe, immigration.
Immigration was the most vital aspect of the work.
Europe was not a place for the Jews.
Europe-- the memories of the displaced persons, especially the adults, was to get to the West, mainly to America.
The Jewish agency, of course, had other plans for the survivors.
Their plan was to get them all to Palestine.
So there was a-- a kind of conflict between social workers like myself [LAUGHS] and the Jewish agency.
Because the Jewish agency felt that Palestine was a future for the Jews.
My social worker's training was that families must be reunited.
And children whose families were in the East, in Poland, must be reunited with their families.
And that was a tremendous conflict between the Jewish agency and myself.
